title: Brent Breaks $90 as US-Iran Strikes Hit Night Nine, US-Iraq Sign $60B Pipeline Bypass, and Ukraine Torches Russian Fuel Chain
subhead: Oil surged past $90 as the US-Iran conflict escalated with a ninth night of strikes and a rising American death toll, while Washington and Baghdad formalized massive pipeline deals to reroute crude around the Strait of Hormuz, and Ukraine's SBU hit three Russian oil depots and a shadow fleet tanker in a single night.

sections.heading: Brent Tops $90 as US-Iran Conflict Enters Ninth Night
sections.body:
Brent crude jumped more than 3% on Monday, surpassing $90 a barrel, as the US-Iran conflict intensified with American forces completing their ninth consecutive night of strikes on Iran. Reuters reports that the escalation renewed inflation fears across Asian markets, which slipped on the day.

The US military confirmed that a service member was killed in northern Iraq on Saturday during a controlled detonation of an unexploded ordnance from a downed Iranian drone, according to CENTCOM. Al Jazeera reports that brings the total American death toll to at least 17 since the conflict began. President Trump stated that Iran had 'lost everything almost militarily,' but offered no clear indication of strategic objectives going forward, according to the Union-Bulletin.

The strikes targeted Iranian command centres, air defence sites, maritime assets, missile facilities, and communications networks. Iranian media reported blasts in cities including Tabriz in the north and Chabahar in the south — the latter a significant port near the Indian Ocean. Meanwhile, only a handful of tankers entered the Strait of Hormuz to load oil over the weekend, according to Reuters data, as shipping companies remain deeply cautious despite the June ceasefire framework. The Philippines confirmed that four of its registered vessels successfully exited Hormuz on Saturday.
sections.whyItMatters: The $90 Brent mark is a psychological and economic threshold. With no clear exit strategy from Washington and tanker traffic still minimal, the market is pricing in an extended disruption rather than a temporary spike.

sections.heading: US-Iraq Sign $60B in Pipeline Deals to Bypass Hormuz
sections.body:
On July 17, US oil companies signed approximately $60 billion in agreements with the Iraqi government, formalized at the US Chamber of Commerce, aimed at building alternative export routes that bypass the Strait of Hormuz entirely.

Chevron signed three preliminary accords covering stakes in two major Iraqi oil fields — West Qurna-2 and Nasiriyah — and is part of a consortium exploring a pipeline route to Syria's Mediterranean coast. The Kirkuk-Baniyas pipeline, which has been out of service for years, would be restored under a US-backed international consortium, according to Tempo.co. ConocoPhillips and Halliburton are also among the firms involved.

The deals emerged from Iraqi Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi's White House visit on July 14, where Trump praised Iraq's oil resources and emphasized expanding American involvement. Al-Zaidi also confirmed that remaining US forces are expected to leave Iraq by September 30.

However, the Jerusalem Post and analysts note significant obstacles. Iran-backed militias and their political allies retain substantial influence in Baghdad. Middle East correspondent Laura Silvia Battaglia told The Media Line that while al-Zaidi may want an 'Iraq first' approach, 'the militias are pushing to preserve their role and to guarantee the continuation of Iran's sphere of influence.' Analyst Tallha Abdulrazaq was blunter: 'Almost everyone holding political power in Iraq today has links to Iran.'
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most significant geopolitical reorientation of Iraqi energy infrastructure in decades — if it can be implemented. The $60B figure and Mediterranean pipeline concept signal that Washington is treating the Hormuz disruption as structural, not temporary. But the deals rest on the assumption that Baghdad can override Tehran-aligned factions, which is far from guaranteed.

sections.heading: Ukraine Strikes Three Russian Oil Depots and a Shadow Fleet Tanker
sections.body:
Ukraine's SBU launched coordinated drone strikes overnight on July 19, targeting three oil depots in Russia's Stavropol region and a Russian shadow fleet tanker in the Black Sea, according to Kyiv Post and the SBU's own reporting.

The Stavropol strikes, approximately 600 km from the Ukrainian border, caused large-scale fires at a Rosneft depot in Viaznyky, a fuel facility on Promyslova Street housing companies Corona and MK-Nefteprodukt, and a Lukoil-Yugnefteprodukt depot with 42 storage tanks and approximately 57,600 cubic meters of capacity storing gasoline, diesel, and lubricants.

Separately, an SBU 'Mamai' surface drone struck the Avero, a Suezmax-class shadow fleet tanker transporting Russian crude to China and India in violation of the G7 oil embargo. This marks the fourth shadow fleet tanker hit by the SBU in ten days, indicating a deliberate campaign to degrade Russia's maritime evasion infrastructure.

Crimea also came under sustained attack. A drone strike and fire were reported at the 110/10 kV Darsan electrical substation in Yalta, with additional explosions in Feodosia, Sudak, and Kerch. Crimea's 2.3 million residents face severe automobile fuel shortages and rolling blackouts after weeks of systematic Ukrainian strikes on fuel storage and logistics.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine is systematically dismantling two layers of Russian energy logistics simultaneously: the domestic fuel chain (depots, refineries) and the export evasion chain (shadow fleet tankers). Four tankers hit in ten days turns the shadow fleet from a sanctions-evasion tool into a liability for Moscow's war financing.

sections.heading: G7 Forms Critical Minerals Alliance, Targets <60% China Dependency by 2030
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, leaders agreed to reduce dependence on any single non-G7 supplier of rare earths and permanent magnets to below 60% by 2030, with a longer-term goal of 50%, Reuters reports. The agreement, signed June 17, establishes a new critical minerals platform with an expanded role for the International Energy Agency to monitor markets and flag distortions.

The initial pilot will cover lithium and nickel, expanding to five new minerals per year with a focus on rare earth elements. G7 countries have announced 195 projects since the start of 2026 totaling €64 billion ($74 billion) in investment. The US launched its $12 billion critical minerals reserve, Project Vault, earlier this year.

However, the CSIS analysis notes that China controls 85-91% of global rare earth refining and 93% of permanent magnet production. Meeting the 60% target will require building midstream and downstream capacity that doesn't yet exist at scale. Japan's experience is instructive: after 15 years of diversification efforts, Japan was still the world's largest importer of Chinese rare earth metals in 2024.

Japanese Prime Minister Shizuka Takaichi pushed a mineral stockpiling plan at the summit, seeking to bridge divides among G7 members, according to SCMP. The G7 committed to boost domestic stockpiling and increase recycling capacity to account for a 'significant share' of annual consumption by 2030.
sections.whyItMatters: The 60% target is a clear political signal, but the gap between ambition and capacity remains enormous. China controls not just mining but processing — and Beijing is already extending its footprint into Malaysia and Indonesia to shape the next generation of supply chains on its terms. The G7's credibility depends on whether Project Vault and allied investments translate into operational refining capacity before 2030.

sections.heading: Japan's $7.5B Louisiana Gas Bet: Mitsubishi Secures LNG Supply Chain
sections.body:
Mitsubishi Corp. finalized a $7.5 billion acquisition of Aethon Energy Management's Haynesville Shale assets this week, marking the latest Japanese multinational to lock down American natural gas production for its LNG supply chain, according to Just the News.

The deal gives Mitsubishi 400,000 gas-producing acres across Louisiana and East Texas, underground pipelines, and an ownership stake in the Cameron LNG export terminal. Combined with JERA's $1.5 billion purchase of the South Mansfield gas field in February and Tokyo Gas's $2.7 billion acquisition of Rockcliff Energy's East Texas assets in 2024, Japanese multinationals will produce an estimated 4.5 billion cubic feet per day — roughly 30-35% of the Haynesville region's total natural gas in 2026.

This trend is underscored by Forbes reporting that the US supplied 93% of global LNG export growth in 2025, with Plaquemines LNG in Louisiana accounting for over 60% of the increase. US LNG exports reached a record 5.2 trillion cubic feet, making America the world's largest LNG exporter at 25.4% global market share. Japan imported 65 million tons of LNG in the fiscal year ending in March, making it the third-largest buyer of American gas after Mexico and South Korea.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan isn't just buying gas — it's buying the entire supply chain, from wellhead to LNG terminal. In a world of energy weaponization, vertically integrating from production through export is the logical hedge. The Haynesville play also means Japanese firms now own the infrastructure closest to Gulf Coast LNG terminals, reducing transport costs and securing priority access when Hormuz disruptions divert cargoes.

sections.heading: China Expands Rare Earth Influence to Malaysia and Indonesia
sections.body:
As the G7 formalizes its critical minerals alliance, Beijing is pursuing state-supervised partnerships in Malaysia and Indonesia to control the next generation of rare earth supply chains, International Business Times reports.

China produced over 90% of the world's refined rare earths in 2023. The IEA estimates that share will drop to roughly 85% in 2025 and 70% by 2035 if planned investments in the US, Malaysia, and elsewhere come online — but China is moving to shape those investments on its terms.

In Malaysia, Beijing offered rare earth processing technology and technical skills in August 2025, with preliminary discussions involving sovereign wealth fund Khazanah Nasional and a Chinese state-owned enterprise about a refinery. Malaysia has also imposed a temporary ban on raw rare earth exports from 2025 through 2027 to encourage domestic processing. Lynas Rare Earths, operating the world's largest rare earth processing facility outside China, is building a 3,000 tonne-per-year NdFeB magnet separation facility near Kuantan with South Korea's JS Link.

In Indonesia, the government announced eight potential rare earth blocks in February 2026 and established state-owned firm Perminas to manage development. No commercial mining or separation operations have been reported yet. China's extensive existing investments in Indonesian nickel processing and battery manufacturing could provide a foundation if rare earth projects advance.

Critical minerals analyst Jack Lifton told Investing News Network: 'China's objective is no longer merely to dominate rare earth production at home. It is to control the global industry by deciding which countries receive the technology, technicians, and market access required to compete. Malaysia and Indonesia are the test cases.'
sections.whyItMatters: The G7's 60% dependency-reduction target assumes alternative supply chains will be independent of Chinese influence. Beijing's strategy — controlling the technology, training, and market access for new processing capacity in Southeast Asia — means that even mines outside China may remain tightly coupled to Chinese decision-making. The battle for rare earths isn't just about where the dirt comes from; it's about who controls the chemistry.

bottomLine: Oil breached $90 as the US-Iran war entered its ninth night with no clear exit, tanker traffic through Hormuz stayed near standstill, and three more American troops died — but the deeper story is the infrastructure race taking shape around the conflict. The $60B US-Iraq pipeline deals, Japan's vertical integration into American gas, and the G7's critical minerals alliance all reflect a world preparing for prolonged supply chain fragmentation. Ukraine's systematic campaign against Russia's shadow fleet and domestic fuel chain adds another layer: the energy war isn't just in the Gulf anymore, it's being fought on three continents simultaneously.
sources: Reuters, Al Jazeera, Kyiv Post, Forbes, Just the News, International Business Times, CSIS, Jerusalem Post/Media Line, SCMP, Tempo.co, Crypto Briefing, Bloomberg, MSN, Union-Bulletin
